commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=6dfc095f7ae984165a4b4ee9d93f3ddfa8dc6763)
commands.review_summary:
The PR changes Gemini `web_search` relative freshness shortcuts from `timeRangeFilter` intervals to query-level recency hints, updates cache partitioning, docs, and focused provider tests.

PR surface: Source 0, Tests +42, Docs +2. Total +44 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence live reproduction is present in this PR. Source inspection shows current main and v2026.6.9 send shortcut freshness as hard `timeRangeFilter`, but the exact Gemini same-day rejection is only asserted narratively here.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Maintainer judgment and contributor proof are needed before changing Gemini freshness semantics; this is not a narrow automation repair until that direction is settled.

target.title: Fix Gemini web search freshness handling
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Gemini-backed `web_search` could fail when callers passed relative `freshness` values like `day`. OpenClaw mapped those shortcuts to Gemini `google_search.timeRangeFilter`, but the Gemini endpoint rejects some relative hard ranges, including the same-day shape seen in production, before any grounded result is returned.

## Why This Change Was Made

This keeps explicit `date_after` / `date_before` behavior as the hard Gemini Google Search grounding time range path, while changing relative `freshness` shortcuts to a query-level recency instruction. That preserves the user's intent to prioritize recent sources without sending an invalid hard filter to Gemini.

The cache key now includes the freshness shortcut even though the hard filter is omitted, so cached responses for the same query remain separated by recency intent.

## User Impact

Users can keep calling Gemini `web_search` with `freshness: "day"`, `week`, `month`, or `year` without the request being rejected by Gemini's time range validation. When a hard grounding range is required, users can still use `date_after` and `date_before`.

## Evidence

Validated on top of `origin/main@65388233e2`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm test:extension google`: 26 test files, 349 tests
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.ts extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.runtime.ts extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts docs/tools/gemini-search.md docs/tools/web.md`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/tools/gemini-search.md docs/tools/web.md`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.plugin-sdk.dts.json --declaration true`
- `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs`

Earlier `codex review --base origin/main` passed with no introduced actionable correctness issues after the schema/docs update.

Live operational validation before this PR: patched Gemini `web_search(freshness=day)` completed successfully on an OpenClaw gateway, with no Gemini `FIELD_INVALID` / `time_range_filter` errors in the post-deploy journal window.

The unrelated lint failures seen in this PR's earlier CI run are split into #95638 instead of being mixed into this Gemini provider change.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: yes. Prepared with Codex. I understand the changed code path: relative Gemini freshness no longer creates a hard `timeRangeFilter`; explicit date ranges still do; and cache partitioning keeps freshness-specific responses separate.
target.author: sunjae-k
